romania.title: Doi alpiniști români au murit în masivul Gran Paradiso din Alpii italieni. Nu se mai știa nimic despre ei din 9 iulie
romania.summary: Doi alpinişti români şi-au pierdut viaţa în masivul Gran Paradiso din Italia după ce au căzut într-o crevasă, au anunţat luni echipele de salvatori italieni, potrivit agenţiei ANSA, citată de Agerpres.

romania.title: Lanțul românesc de șaormerii Dristor Kebap anunță că a strâns 1,25 milioane EUR de la investitorii de pe Seedblink și și-a luat consilier un fost director regional al Unilever
romania.summary: Lanțul românesc de șaormerii Dristor Kebap anunță, luni, într-un comunicat transmis comunității antreprenoriale StartupCafe.ro, că a strâns deja 1,25 milioane EUR de la investitorii de pe platforma de multifinanțare SeedBlink și că a cooptat drept consilier un fost director regional al colosului internațional din industria bunurilor de larg consum Unilever.

romania.title: The Coffee Village Festival revine la Cluj cu trei zile dedicate cafelei de specialitate / 14-16 august 2026 | Iulius Parc
romania.summary: În perioada 14-16 august 2026, Iulius Parc din Cluj-Napoca devine, din nou, locul de întâlnire al iubitorilor de cafea. După o primă ediție care a adunat mii de vizitatori, The… Articolul The Coffee Village Festival revine la Cluj cu trei zile dedicate cafelei de specialitate / 14-16 august 2026 | Iulius Parc apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: Pe fondul unei cereri din ce în ce mai reduse de maşini second-hand pe pieţe precum România, Albania sau Burkina Faso, conducerea Volkswagen e nevoită să ia măsuri dure! Directorul general al companiei, Pimmel Schwanzhoff, a anunţat recent că va disponibiliza aproape 50.000 de bătrânici care scoteau maşinile din garaj duminica pentru a merge doar […] The post Piaţa maşinilor second, în scădere. VW concediază 50.000 de bătrânici care conduceau doar duminica appeared first on Times New Roman .

Traducerea este semnată de… Articolul Romanul „Oameni mari”, de Maria Orban, publicat în limba franceză apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Uneori,… Articolul „VALENTIN M-A TREZIT! – CORPUS”. O expoziție de Noni-Emil Iordache apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: Protest la Minsterul Educației. Mii de elevi de clasa a XII-a, care au picat BAC-ul, solicită o lege care să le dea dreptul să se înscrie la doctorat fără diploma de Bacalaurea. Ei spun că vor să facă cercetare și statul nu le permite, pentru că are legi învechite, care nu stimulează performanța academică. Mulți […] The post Elevii care au picat BAC-ul cer să poată intra la doctorat și fără BAC appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 Un bătrân care urla pe stradă a fost confundat cu Dan Negru
romania.summary: Un bătrân cu vizibile probleme mintale, care urla din toți rărunchii pe strada principală din Scrioaștea, Teleorman, a fost confundat cu Dan Negru. Sătenii s-au adunat imediat în jurul lui, convinși că urmează să audă încă o pildă despre cum pe vremuri se muncea mai cu spor, roșiile aveau gust, iar copiii nu stăteau pe […] The post Un bătrân care urla pe stradă a fost confundat cu Dan Negru appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas
romania.summary: Descoperă povestea Alinei și cum sprijinul oferit de Fundația Inocenți a ajutat-o să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile școlare și curajul de a reuși. The post Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

romania.summary: București, 1 iulie. Rezultatele Evaluării Naționale 2026 reconfirmă decalajele profunde dintre copiii din mediul rural și cei din mediul urban... Articolul Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

romania.title: Sondaj World Vision România de Ziua Copilului: aproape un sfert dintre copii și adolescenți consultați spun că adulții îi ascultă rar sau aproape niciodată. Anxietatea și presiunea școlii, principalele probleme ale generației lor.
romania.summary: București, 1 iunie 2026. Aproape un sfert dintre adolescenții consultați de World Vision România spun că adulții îi ascultă rar... Articolul Sondaj World Vision România de Ziua Copilului: aproape un sfert dintre copii și adolescenți consultați spun că adulții îi ascultă rar sau aproape niciodată. Anxietatea și presiunea școlii, principalele probleme ale generației lor. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

medical.title: Can infants detect voices in noisy environments? New research says yes
medical.summary: In noisy environments, organisms differentiate sounds they want to detect from interfering noise to improve their perception of target sounds. This process is widely conserved across species, including birds, crocodiles, ferrets and human adults. But how early in life does this ability emerge? In a new JNeurosci paper, Farhin Ahmed, Qianxun Zheng and colleagues at the University of Washington explored whether infants also use this evolutionarily conserved mechanism to detect sounds in noisy environments.

medical.title: Researchers reveal immune mechanisms that drive development of rare allergic stomach disease
medical.summary: Researchers at Tel Aviv University have developed one of the first experimental models that faithfully reproduces eosinophilic gastritis (EoG), a rare but increasingly recognized allergic disease of the stomach. Using this model, they identified the immune pathways responsible for driving the disease and explained why a new generation of biologic therapies currently being evaluated in clinical trials may benefit patients.

medical.title: Regenerating tissues may rebuild order by amplifying tiny cell differences
medical.summary: FMI researchers and their collaborators have shown how regenerating intestinal tissue turns small initial differences between cells into stable patterns. The findings reveal a general principle for how tissues rebuild order after injury, with possible implications for regenerative medicine.

medical.title: Long COVID patients are told symptoms are in their head—here's how to change the narrative
medical.summary: Between us, we bring two perspectives to persistent illness: personal experience of long COVID and clinical and research experience in chronic illness rehabilitation.

medical.title: Two-year lifestyle program improves older adults' cognition 55% more, trial finds
medical.summary: Older adults who followed a structured, 2-year healthy lifestyle program improved their memory and thinking skills significantly more than those who received only general health advice, according to the Latin American Initiative for Lifestyle Intervention to Prevent Cognitive Decline (LatAm-FINGERS) trial, published in The Lancet. Gains were about 55% greater per year on overall tests of memory and thinking.

medical.title: Keeping moms in the game: FIFA launches guide for pregnant and post-partum players
medical.summary: Soccer players who are pregnant or have recently given birth can kick off the season with more confidence, using a new FIFA tool developed in collaboration with a University of Alberta researcher. Two decision aids, one for playing during pregnancy and one for returning to the game postpartum, have been launched by FIFA, an international governing body for the sport. Both are published in the British Journal of Sports Medicine.

medical.title: New insights into neuron–microglia interactions during brain aging
medical.summary: Researchers at the University of Tsukuba have identified small nuclear fragments known as micronuclei while studying neurons in aged mice. They demonstrated that microglia—the brain's resident immune cells—take up these micronuclei, leading to changes in microglial morphology and gene expression associated with inflammation and vascular function. The research is published in the journal Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience.

medical.title: Contact-free sensors could help track movement symptoms in Parkinson's disease
medical.summary: Disease progression in people with Parkinson's disease can be accurately monitored using sensors, removing some of the limitations of wearable technologies, a new study reports. In the study published in the Journal of NeuroEngineering and Rehabilitation, scientists from the UK Dementia Research Institute (UKDRI) Care Research & Technology Center, of which the University of Surrey is a key member, sought to develop a more accurate, zero-burden way to track movement changes in people affected by Parkinson's.

medical.title: Can cosmetic procedures become addictive? Study links addiction risk to social media and body image
medical.summary: As cosmetic procedures surge worldwide and beauty content increasingly dominates social media platforms, a new study from the Hebrew University of Jerusalem suggests that for some women, cosmetic treatments may begin to resemble compulsive or addiction-like behavior. The study, led by Dr. Vera Skvirsky alongside Dr. Uri Lifshin, Dr. Dvora Shmulewitz and Prof. Mario Mikulincer from the Department of Psychology at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem and the Israel Center for Addiction and Mental Health (ICAMH), examined what the researchers describe as "addictive cosmetic procedure use," or ACPU, among Jewish Israeli women.

medical.title: Ureters stay visible for hours with dye designed to vanish through kidneys
medical.summary: A cross-disciplinary research team led by Hongjie Dai, director of the Materials Institute of Life Sciences and Energy (MILES) at the University of Hong Kong (HKU) in Shenzhen, has developed a promising near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent dye for potential clinical use in ureter imaging during surgical navigation.

medical.title: Heat exposure during pregnancy and infancy may influence children's brain development
medical.summary: Exposure to high temperatures during pregnancy and early infancy is associated with slower growth of the thalamus later in childhood, according to a study led by the Barcelona Institute for Global Health (ISGlobal), a center supported by the "la Caixa" Foundation. The findings, published in Environment International, suggest that heat exposure during the earliest stages of life may have lasting effects on brain development.

medical.title: Secondhand smoke independently disrupts children's sleep
medical.summary: A new study from Ben-Gurion University of the Negev (BGU) has found that children exposed to secondhand smoke have significantly poorer sleep quality and greater sleep fragmentation, independent of the severity of their breathing problems. The prospective clinical trial, published Monday in the journal Scientific Reports, uses objective sleep-lab data and biological markers to demonstrate that tobacco smoke directly disrupts pediatric sleep per se, rather than simply worsening existing airway blockages.

medical.title: Patients whose physicians scored higher in LKA less likely to receive low-value care
medical.summary: Patients whose physicians scored higher in the first year of the American Board of Internal Medicine's (ABIM) Internal Medicine Longitudinal Knowledge Assessment (LKA) were significantly less likely to receive low-value health care services, which cumulatively cost the U.S. health care system an estimated $100 billion each year. The authors say the findings, published today in JAMA Internal Medicine, highlight an association between greater physician knowledge and lower use of tests and interventions that provide little to no benefit to patients.

medical.title: Trauma triage is challenging: Study assesses how AI might help improve accuracy
medical.summary: Making triage decisions in a busy emergency room is tough enough; making triage decisions based on a hurried call with emergency medical staff responding at the scene of an accident is all the more challenging.

medical.title: Expert urges redesign for aged care systems
medical.summary: Experts are calling for a major overhaul of health and aged care systems to accommodate rising numbers of older people.

medical.title: New method separates brain signals to show how complex actions are organized
medical.summary: A new Northwestern Medicine study has introduced a novel machine learning method for analyzing how the brain organizes complex behaviors, offering fresh evidence that neural activity is built from reusable "building blocks." The study is published in Neuron.

medical.title: Neck pain at work may stem from poor sleep, stress and high workload—not just bad posture, AI study shows
medical.summary: Artificial intelligence could predict an individual office worker's risk of musculoskeletal injury in specific body parts, a study by QUT health and data scientists has found.

medical.title: The brains of blind people reorganize differently than previously thought
medical.summary: The development of the human brain is one of the most complex biological processes. During the first years of life, enormous changes take place in the brain that help optimize its functioning. In the first year alone, the surface area of the cerebral cortex increases by about three-quarters, while its thickness grows by roughly one-third. At the beginning of development, the brain creates far more connections between neurons than it actually needs. Over the course of life, frequently used connections are strengthened, while others are eliminated in a process called pruning.

medical.title: Love unplugged: What happened when I swapped apps for dating in real life
medical.summary: Burnt out by the apps, Angela Garwood took her quest for love offline. Here’s how she got on The post Love unplugged: What happened when I swapped apps for dating in real life appeared first on Positive News .

science.title: Hidden in plain sight: Caribbean reef fish nestle in tube worms, revealing previously undocumented partnership
science.summary: On Caribbean coral reefs, an unlikely partnership has gone largely unnoticed: Tiny fish regularly nestle within the feathery structures of tube worms. While these sensitive worms typically snap shut at the slightest disturbance, they show a remarkable tolerance for their tiny guests.

science.title: Brazil's highland forest has been shaped by climate change and Indigenous people for 6,000 years
science.summary: When you think of a South American rainforest, you probably don't imagine biting winds, heavy frosts and freezing temperatures. But in the mountains of southern Brazil, that's exactly what you can find. On this highland plateau, far from Amazonia in the country's coldest region, grows one of the world's most intriguing ecosystems.

science.title: Theorization of environmental justice in Chinese political philosophy
science.summary: Shizhi Zhang, Linda Westman and Vanesa Castán Broto have published a paper in Political Geography that explores how classical Chinese political philosophy can contribute to contemporary debates on environmental justice (EJ) theory.

science.title: Wealthier and more populated metropolitan areas respond more strongly to early drought news by saving water
science.summary: As climate change increases the risk of severe droughts, water resources management is an urgent challenge. Drought develops slowly, which can make it difficult for the public to recognize an ongoing drought. Governments rely on the media to communicate drought risks and encourage water conservation. However, it has remained unclear how much drought-related news actually contributes to water-saving behavior in different regions.

science.title: Study reveals how gas bubbles shaped Kīlauea's 2018 lava flow
science.summary: The lava that buried entire neighborhoods during the 2018 Kīlauea eruption was composed of nearly 80% gas bubbles near its source. A recent study shows that those bubbles played a central role in controlling how fast and far the lava traveled and that lava flow models need to account for bubbles to more accurately forecast where lava will stop.

science.title: Atoms tell different stories when light hits a molecule in trillionths of a second
science.summary: Researchers have captured how a molecule redistributes energy after absorbing light, differentiating the roles of individual atoms in the process. They used X-ray flashes from the European XFEL to show that different atoms in the same molecule can reveal different aspects of the process. The study provides evidence that excitation by light can enhance an atom's sensitivity to the motion of nearby atoms. The new method for following ultrafast chemical reactions at the atomic scale, in real time, can help researchers understand photostability in DNA, energy flow in light-harvesting materials and other fundamental processes driven by light.

science.title: Big bees have the most to lose as global CO₂ levels rise: New research
science.summary: Pollinators—including bees, flies, wasps, moths, butterflies and some nectar-loving birds—are a cornerstone of our natural environment. By helping plants reproduce, they keep our ecosystems healthy and ensure we can grow vital food crops.

science.title: Chemists make elusive carbon-bridged sandwich molecule once thought too strained to exist
science.summary: Progress in chemistry is often gradual, with some of its most important advances taking years—sometimes decades—to unfold. A case in point is the discovery of a novel "ferrocenophane" from the class of compounds known as "sandwich molecules"—so named because of their particular structure. In a ferrocenophane, the "bread slices" are two carbon rings that enclose an iron atom as the sandwich "filling." A team of chemists at Saarland University has now succeeded in developing a highly unusual bent sandwich molecule that opens up new possibilities for designing iron-containing materials.

science.title: Widely-used method for assessing stream health doesn't work very well
science.summary: A new study finds a widely used technique for assessing the health of freshwater streams is not effective at detecting a range of water quality problems, including those related to acidity, oxygen levels and the presence of pathogens.

science.title: 3D-printable elastic polymer proves surprisingly strong and durable
science.summary: EPFL researchers have discovered that a soft material originally optimized for 3D printing may solve a longstanding challenge in materials science: making 3D-printable elastomers both tough and durable.

science.title: The 2026 World Cup's header trends: More assists, higher precision
science.summary: Headers have accounted for a shrinking percentage of World Cup goals, dropping from 23.7% in 2018 to 18.4% in 2022 to 17.9% in 2026 through the round of 16, according to Northeastern's NetSI Sport research group, which is analyzing this year's tournament data. However, headers are becoming more accurate—converting at a higher rate (11.4%) and producing more shots on target (33.5%) than in the two previous World Cups.

science.title: NASA Astronaut Anil Menon
science.summary: NASA astronaut Anil Menon poses in a spacesuit for a portrait at NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston, Texas on Jan. 8, 2026. Menon will launch aboard the Roscosmos Soyuz MS-29 spacecraft to the International Space Station on Tuesday, July 14, accompanied by cosmonauts Pyotr Dubrov and Anna Kikina, where they will join the Expedition […]

science.title: The US just approved a giant space mirror to test 'sunlight on demand.' Low Earth orbit is getting weird
science.summary: A giant mirror to create "sunlight on demand" was just approved by the United States Federal Communications Commission (FCC), despite opposition from astronomers and the public, and real safety concerns.

science.title: Study reports the first detection of a sugar in interstellar space
science.summary: Sugars are key biomolecules in living organisms, as they form the backbone of DNA and RNA and play a fundamental role in metabolic processes. In theories of the origin of life, sugars are also essential for the synthesis of the first nucleic acids. Despite their importance, one of the major questions in origin-of-life research is how the first sugars formed on Earth, since laboratory experiments show that they do not form in sufficient quantities under prebiotic conditions.

science.title: Uncovering the evidence for child abuse and neglect in archaeological remains
science.summary: A team of researchers from the University of Aberdeen has developed a new framework for identifying the often-subtle signs of child abuse and neglect in antiquity. Previous osteoarchaeological studies have tended to focus on skeletal signs of severe physical injury, but this new research focuses on more subtle injuries and child neglect.

science.title: Solving a 30-year-old puzzle about a mysterious superconducting material
science.summary: A material made from yttrium, barium and copper oxide (better known as YBCO) has intrigued scientists since its discovery in 1987, largely because it retains its superconductive properties at a higher-than-normal temperature. However, it is extremely brittle, which makes it tricky to put to practical use.

science.title: Biosensor detects nanoplastics in water within minutes
science.summary: The growing presence of nanoplastics in the environment has highlighted the need for simpler detection methods, leading researchers at Science Tokyo to develop a rapid biosensor for detecting polystyrene nanoparticles in water. Tested in both model water samples and real water samples spiked with polystyrene nanoparticles, the device detected 50 nm particles within 20 minutes without labeling or extensive sample preparation. The technology could support efforts to understand and address nanoplastic accumulation in the environment.

science.title: 2.5 million stem cells reveal first genome-scale guide to gene function
science.summary: A team led by bioengineers at the University of California San Diego has developed a genome-scale reference map that details how individual genes control the functions and identities of human stem cells. This open-access resource could help researchers build virtual cell models for complex diseases, as well as design patient-specific treatments for these diseases.

science.title: 3,400-year-old gold diadems and mouth-pieces from Cyprus blend the art of Egypt, Greece and the Near East
science.summary: Buried in the rubble outside an ancient city, archaeologists have discovered golden diadems and mouthpieces stamped with sun-crowned bulls and running ibexes. Their designs borrow from nearly every corner of the ancient Mediterranean, providing a snapshot of ancient trade in one of history's first great ages of globalization.

science.title: The ball is round—and contrary to some keepers' views, in this World Cup it has performed just fine
science.summary: Not every World Cup goal is a classic. Sometimes a half-hearted shot goes in as a result of little more than a goalkeeper's error. And on those occasions, goalies may be inclined to find an excuse.

science.title: NASA’s Hubble Discovers First of Star Cluster’s Missing Black Holes
science.summary: Astronomers using archival data from Hubble and supportive observations from Webb have located their first stellar-mass black hole in the star cluster Omega Centauri.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: 😂 Un bătrân care urla pe stradă a fost confundat cu Dan Negru
mix_items.summary: Un bătrân cu vizibile probleme mintale, care urla din toți rărunchii pe strada principală din Scrioaștea, Teleorman, a fost confundat cu Dan Negru. Sătenii s-au adunat imediat în jurul lui, convinși că urmează să audă încă o pildă despre cum pe vremuri se muncea mai cu spor, roșiile aveau gust, iar copiii nu stăteau pe […] The post Un bătrân care urla pe stradă a fost confundat cu Dan Negru appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Doi alpiniști români au murit în masivul Gran Paradiso din Alpii italieni. Nu se mai știa nimic despre ei din 9 iulie
mix_items.summary: Doi alpinişti români şi-au pierdut viaţa în masivul Gran Paradiso din Italia după ce au căzut într-o crevasă, au anunţat luni echipele de salvatori italieni, potrivit agenţiei ANSA, citată de Agerpres.

mix_items.title: Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Descoperă povestea Alinei și cum sprijinul oferit de Fundația Inocenți a ajutat-o să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile școlare și curajul de a reuși. The post Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție
mix_items.summary: București, 1 iulie. Rezultatele Evaluării Naționale 2026 reconfirmă decalajele profunde dintre copiii din mediul rural și cei din mediul urban... Articolul Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Părinte Social (Mamă SOS) – București și Cisnădie, Sibiu Transformă un loc de muncă într-o misiune de viață La SOS Satele Copiilor România, credem că fiecare copil are dreptul să... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Sondaj World Vision România de Ziua Copilului: aproape un sfert dintre copii și adolescenți consultați spun că adulții îi ascultă rar sau aproape niciodată. Anxietatea și presiunea școlii, principalele probleme ale generației lor.
mix_items.summary: București, 1 iunie 2026. Aproape un sfert dintre adolescenții consultați de World Vision România spun că adulții îi ascultă rar... Articolul Sondaj World Vision România de Ziua Copilului: aproape un sfert dintre copii și adolescenți consultați spun că adulții îi ascultă rar sau aproape niciodată. Anxietatea și presiunea școlii, principalele probleme ale generației lor. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Hidden in plain sight: Caribbean reef fish nestle in tube worms, revealing previously undocumented partnership
mix_items.summary: On Caribbean coral reefs, an unlikely partnership has gone largely unnoticed: Tiny fish regularly nestle within the feathery structures of tube worms. While these sensitive worms typically snap shut at the slightest disturbance, they show a remarkable tolerance for their tiny guests.

mix_items.title: Can infants detect voices in noisy environments? New research says yes
mix_items.summary: In noisy environments, organisms differentiate sounds they want to detect from interfering noise to improve their perception of target sounds. This process is widely conserved across species, including birds, crocodiles, ferrets and human adults. But how early in life does this ability emerge? In a new JNeurosci paper, Farhin Ahmed, Qianxun Zheng and colleagues at the University of Washington explored whether infants also use this evolutionarily conserved mechanism to detect sounds in noisy environments.

mix_items.title: Researchers reveal immune mechanisms that drive development of rare allergic stomach disease
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Tel Aviv University have developed one of the first experimental models that faithfully reproduces eosinophilic gastritis (EoG), a rare but increasingly recognized allergic disease of the stomach. Using this model, they identified the immune pathways responsible for driving the disease and explained why a new generation of biologic therapies currently being evaluated in clinical trials may benefit patients.

mix_items.title: Brazil's highland forest has been shaped by climate change and Indigenous people for 6,000 years
mix_items.summary: When you think of a South American rainforest, you probably don't imagine biting winds, heavy frosts and freezing temperatures. But in the mountains of southern Brazil, that's exactly what you can find. On this highland plateau, far from Amazonia in the country's coldest region, grows one of the world's most intriguing ecosystems.

mix_items.title: Theorization of environmental justice in Chinese political philosophy
mix_items.summary: Shizhi Zhang, Linda Westman and Vanesa Castán Broto have published a paper in Political Geography that explores how classical Chinese political philosophy can contribute to contemporary debates on environmental justice (EJ) theory.

mix_items.title: Regenerating tissues may rebuild order by amplifying tiny cell differences
mix_items.summary: FMI researchers and their collaborators have shown how regenerating intestinal tissue turns small initial differences between cells into stable patterns. The findings reveal a general principle for how tissues rebuild order after injury, with possible implications for regenerative medicine.

mix_items.title: Long COVID patients are told symptoms are in their head—here's how to change the narrative
mix_items.summary: Between us, we bring two perspectives to persistent illness: personal experience of long COVID and clinical and research experience in chronic illness rehabilitation.

mix_items.title: Two-year lifestyle program improves older adults' cognition 55% more, trial finds
mix_items.summary: Older adults who followed a structured, 2-year healthy lifestyle program improved their memory and thinking skills significantly more than those who received only general health advice, according to the Latin American Initiative for Lifestyle Intervention to Prevent Cognitive Decline (LatAm-FINGERS) trial, published in The Lancet. Gains were about 55% greater per year on overall tests of memory and thinking.

mix_items.title: Wealthier and more populated metropolitan areas respond more strongly to early drought news by saving water
mix_items.summary: As climate change increases the risk of severe droughts, water resources management is an urgent challenge. Drought develops slowly, which can make it difficult for the public to recognize an ongoing drought. Governments rely on the media to communicate drought risks and encourage water conservation. However, it has remained unclear how much drought-related news actually contributes to water-saving behavior in different regions.

mix_items.title: Study reveals how gas bubbles shaped Kīlauea's 2018 lava flow
mix_items.summary: The lava that buried entire neighborhoods during the 2018 Kīlauea eruption was composed of nearly 80% gas bubbles near its source. A recent study shows that those bubbles played a central role in controlling how fast and far the lava traveled and that lava flow models need to account for bubbles to more accurately forecast where lava will stop.

mix_items.title: Keeping moms in the game: FIFA launches guide for pregnant and post-partum players
mix_items.summary: Soccer players who are pregnant or have recently given birth can kick off the season with more confidence, using a new FIFA tool developed in collaboration with a University of Alberta researcher. Two decision aids, one for playing during pregnancy and one for returning to the game postpartum, have been launched by FIFA, an international governing body for the sport. Both are published in the British Journal of Sports Medicine.

mix_items.title: New insights into neuron–microglia interactions during brain aging
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the University of Tsukuba have identified small nuclear fragments known as micronuclei while studying neurons in aged mice. They demonstrated that microglia—the brain's resident immune cells—take up these micronuclei, leading to changes in microglial morphology and gene expression associated with inflammation and vascular function. The research is published in the journal Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience.

mix_items.title: Contact-free sensors could help track movement symptoms in Parkinson's disease
mix_items.summary: Disease progression in people with Parkinson's disease can be accurately monitored using sensors, removing some of the limitations of wearable technologies, a new study reports. In the study published in the Journal of NeuroEngineering and Rehabilitation, scientists from the UK Dementia Research Institute (UKDRI) Care Research & Technology Center, of which the University of Surrey is a key member, sought to develop a more accurate, zero-burden way to track movement changes in people affected by Parkinson's.

mix_items.title: Atoms tell different stories when light hits a molecule in trillionths of a second
mix_items.summary: Researchers have captured how a molecule redistributes energy after absorbing light, differentiating the roles of individual atoms in the process. They used X-ray flashes from the European XFEL to show that different atoms in the same molecule can reveal different aspects of the process. The study provides evidence that excitation by light can enhance an atom's sensitivity to the motion of nearby atoms. The new method for following ultrafast chemical reactions at the atomic scale, in real time, can help researchers understand photostability in DNA, energy flow in light-harvesting materials and other fundamental processes driven by light.

mix_items.title: Big bees have the most to lose as global CO₂ levels rise: New research
mix_items.summary: Pollinators—including bees, flies, wasps, moths, butterflies and some nectar-loving birds—are a cornerstone of our natural environment. By helping plants reproduce, they keep our ecosystems healthy and ensure we can grow vital food crops.

mix_items.title: Chemists make elusive carbon-bridged sandwich molecule once thought too strained to exist
mix_items.summary: Progress in chemistry is often gradual, with some of its most important advances taking years—sometimes decades—to unfold. A case in point is the discovery of a novel "ferrocenophane" from the class of compounds known as "sandwich molecules"—so named because of their particular structure. In a ferrocenophane, the "bread slices" are two carbon rings that enclose an iron atom as the sandwich "filling." A team of chemists at Saarland University has now succeeded in developing a highly unusual bent sandwich molecule that opens up new possibilities for designing iron-containing materials.

mix_items.title: Can cosmetic procedures become addictive? Study links addiction risk to social media and body image
mix_items.summary: As cosmetic procedures surge worldwide and beauty content increasingly dominates social media platforms, a new study from the Hebrew University of Jerusalem suggests that for some women, cosmetic treatments may begin to resemble compulsive or addiction-like behavior. The study, led by Dr. Vera Skvirsky alongside Dr. Uri Lifshin, Dr. Dvora Shmulewitz and Prof. Mario Mikulincer from the Department of Psychology at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem and the Israel Center for Addiction and Mental Health (ICAMH), examined what the researchers describe as "addictive cosmetic procedure use," or ACPU, among Jewish Israeli women.

mix_items.title: Widely-used method for assessing stream health doesn't work very well
mix_items.summary: A new study finds a widely used technique for assessing the health of freshwater streams is not effective at detecting a range of water quality problems, including those related to acidity, oxygen levels and the presence of pathogens.

mix_items.title: Ureters stay visible for hours with dye designed to vanish through kidneys
mix_items.summary: A cross-disciplinary research team led by Hongjie Dai, director of the Materials Institute of Life Sciences and Energy (MILES) at the University of Hong Kong (HKU) in Shenzhen, has developed a promising near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent dye for potential clinical use in ureter imaging during surgical navigation.

mix_items.title: 3D-printable elastic polymer proves surprisingly strong and durable
mix_items.summary: EPFL researchers have discovered that a soft material originally optimized for 3D printing may solve a longstanding challenge in materials science: making 3D-printable elastomers both tough and durable.

mix_items.title: The 2026 World Cup's header trends: More assists, higher precision
mix_items.summary: Headers have accounted for a shrinking percentage of World Cup goals, dropping from 23.7% in 2018 to 18.4% in 2022 to 17.9% in 2026 through the round of 16, according to Northeastern's NetSI Sport research group, which is analyzing this year's tournament data. However, headers are becoming more accurate—converting at a higher rate (11.4%) and producing more shots on target (33.5%) than in the two previous World Cups.

mix_items.title: Heat exposure during pregnancy and infancy may influence children's brain development
mix_items.summary: Exposure to high temperatures during pregnancy and early infancy is associated with slower growth of the thalamus later in childhood, according to a study led by the Barcelona Institute for Global Health (ISGlobal), a center supported by the "la Caixa" Foundation. The findings, published in Environment International, suggest that heat exposure during the earliest stages of life may have lasting effects on brain development.

mix_items.title: NASA Astronaut Anil Menon
mix_items.summary: NASA astronaut Anil Menon poses in a spacesuit for a portrait at NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston, Texas on Jan. 8, 2026. Menon will launch aboard the Roscosmos Soyuz MS-29 spacecraft to the International Space Station on Tuesday, July 14, accompanied by cosmonauts Pyotr Dubrov and Anna Kikina, where they will join the Expedition […]

mix_items.title: Secondhand smoke independently disrupts children's sleep
mix_items.summary: A new study from Ben-Gurion University of the Negev (BGU) has found that children exposed to secondhand smoke have significantly poorer sleep quality and greater sleep fragmentation, independent of the severity of their breathing problems. The prospective clinical trial, published Monday in the journal Scientific Reports, uses objective sleep-lab data and biological markers to demonstrate that tobacco smoke directly disrupts pediatric sleep per se, rather than simply worsening existing airway blockages.

mix_items.title: The US just approved a giant space mirror to test 'sunlight on demand.' Low Earth orbit is getting weird
mix_items.summary: A giant mirror to create "sunlight on demand" was just approved by the United States Federal Communications Commission (FCC), despite opposition from astronomers and the public, and real safety concerns.

mix_items.title: Patients whose physicians scored higher in LKA less likely to receive low-value care
mix_items.summary: Patients whose physicians scored higher in the first year of the American Board of Internal Medicine's (ABIM) Internal Medicine Longitudinal Knowledge Assessment (LKA) were significantly less likely to receive low-value health care services, which cumulatively cost the U.S. health care system an estimated $100 billion each year. The authors say the findings, published today in JAMA Internal Medicine, highlight an association between greater physician knowledge and lower use of tests and interventions that provide little to no benefit to patients.

mix_items.title: Study reports the first detection of a sugar in interstellar space
mix_items.summary: Sugars are key biomolecules in living organisms, as they form the backbone of DNA and RNA and play a fundamental role in metabolic processes. In theories of the origin of life, sugars are also essential for the synthesis of the first nucleic acids. Despite their importance, one of the major questions in origin-of-life research is how the first sugars formed on Earth, since laboratory experiments show that they do not form in sufficient quantities under prebiotic conditions.

mix_items.title: Trauma triage is challenging: Study assesses how AI might help improve accuracy
mix_items.summary: Making triage decisions in a busy emergency room is tough enough; making triage decisions based on a hurried call with emergency medical staff responding at the scene of an accident is all the more challenging.

mix_items.title: Uncovering the evidence for child abuse and neglect in archaeological remains
mix_items.summary: A team of researchers from the University of Aberdeen has developed a new framework for identifying the often-subtle signs of child abuse and neglect in antiquity. Previous osteoarchaeological studies have tended to focus on skeletal signs of severe physical injury, but this new research focuses on more subtle injuries and child neglect.

mix_items.title: Solving a 30-year-old puzzle about a mysterious superconducting material
mix_items.summary: A material made from yttrium, barium and copper oxide (better known as YBCO) has intrigued scientists since its discovery in 1987, largely because it retains its superconductive properties at a higher-than-normal temperature. However, it is extremely brittle, which makes it tricky to put to practical use.

mix_items.title: Expert urges redesign for aged care systems
mix_items.summary: Experts are calling for a major overhaul of health and aged care systems to accommodate rising numbers of older people.

mix_items.title: New method separates brain signals to show how complex actions are organized
mix_items.summary: A new Northwestern Medicine study has introduced a novel machine learning method for analyzing how the brain organizes complex behaviors, offering fresh evidence that neural activity is built from reusable "building blocks." The study is published in Neuron.

mix_items.title: Biosensor detects nanoplastics in water within minutes
mix_items.summary: The growing presence of nanoplastics in the environment has highlighted the need for simpler detection methods, leading researchers at Science Tokyo to develop a rapid biosensor for detecting polystyrene nanoparticles in water. Tested in both model water samples and real water samples spiked with polystyrene nanoparticles, the device detected 50 nm particles within 20 minutes without labeling or extensive sample preparation. The technology could support efforts to understand and address nanoplastic accumulation in the environment.

mix_items.title: Neck pain at work may stem from poor sleep, stress and high workload—not just bad posture, AI study shows
mix_items.summary: Artificial intelligence could predict an individual office worker's risk of musculoskeletal injury in specific body parts, a study by QUT health and data scientists has found.

mix_items.title: 2.5 million stem cells reveal first genome-scale guide to gene function
mix_items.summary: A team led by bioengineers at the University of California San Diego has developed a genome-scale reference map that details how individual genes control the functions and identities of human stem cells. This open-access resource could help researchers build virtual cell models for complex diseases, as well as design patient-specific treatments for these diseases.

mix_items.title: 3,400-year-old gold diadems and mouth-pieces from Cyprus blend the art of Egypt, Greece and the Near East
mix_items.summary: Buried in the rubble outside an ancient city, archaeologists have discovered golden diadems and mouthpieces stamped with sun-crowned bulls and running ibexes. Their designs borrow from nearly every corner of the ancient Mediterranean, providing a snapshot of ancient trade in one of history's first great ages of globalization.

mix_items.title: The brains of blind people reorganize differently than previously thought
mix_items.summary: The development of the human brain is one of the most complex biological processes. During the first years of life, enormous changes take place in the brain that help optimize its functioning. In the first year alone, the surface area of the cerebral cortex increases by about three-quarters, while its thickness grows by roughly one-third. At the beginning of development, the brain creates far more connections between neurons than it actually needs. Over the course of life, frequently used connections are strengthened, while others are eliminated in a process called pruning.

mix_items.title: The ball is round—and contrary to some keepers' views, in this World Cup it has performed just fine
mix_items.summary: Not every World Cup goal is a classic. Sometimes a half-hearted shot goes in as a result of little more than a goalkeeper's error. And on those occasions, goalies may be inclined to find an excuse.

mix_items.title: NASA’s Hubble Discovers First of Star Cluster’s Missing Black Holes
mix_items.summary: Astronomers using archival data from Hubble and supportive observations from Webb have located their first stellar-mass black hole in the star cluster Omega Centauri.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: Love unplugged: What happened when I swapped apps for dating in real life
mix_items.summary: Burnt out by the apps, Angela Garwood took her quest for love offline. Here’s how she got on The post Love unplugged: What happened when I swapped apps for dating in real life appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Azi sunt foarte zen. N-am energie nici sa ma stresez.

satire_ro.title: Piaţa maşinilor second, în scădere. VW concediază 50.000 de bătrânici care conduceau doar duminica
